meta.name: Unstable Wide-Complex Tachycardia — Monomorphic VT, Sedation, Synchronized Cardioversion

patient.name: Diana Castellano
patient.chiefComplaint: Sudden palpitations, chest pressure, and progressive lightheadedness/confusion that began about 25 minutes ago while sitting at her desk; nearly slumped from her chair before coworkers caught her.

history.hpi: 56-year-old female office manager was sitting at her desk reviewing payroll when she suddenly felt her heart 'racing and pounding hard.' Within minutes she became diaphoretic, developed substernal chest pressure rated 6/10, and felt severely lightheaded. A coworker noted her becoming pale and confused — answering questions slowly and asking the same question twice — and called 911. Patient has a history of an inferior STEMI 4 years ago (treated with primary PCI and a single stent to the RCA) and known left ventricular dysfunction (EF 35%) with documented non-sustained VT on a recent Holter, for which she was scheduled to see her electrophysiologist next week. She has been taking her metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily and amiodarone 200 mg daily as prescribed. Denies fever, recent illness, missed doses, recent stimulant or caffeine binge, or known electrolyte issues.
pastMedical: Inferior STEMI 2022 with single-vessel PCI (RCA stent)
Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 35%)
Documented non-sustained ventricular tachycardia on Holter monitor (last month)
history.lastOralIntake: Coffee and a yogurt about 2 hours ago
history.events: Sudden palpitations and chest pressure ~25 minutes ago at work; progressive diaphoresis, lightheadedness, and confusion; nearly fell from her chair. Coworkers called 911 and laid her flat on the floor. No witnessed loss of consciousness, no trauma.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 56-year-old female, complaint of rapid heart rate and chest pain at a downtown office. Conscious but confused per coworkers. ALS response.
scene.location: Open-plan office on the 4th floor of a downtown building. Patient lying supine on the carpet beside her desk; coworkers cleared a path. Elevator available with stretcher access.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Coworkers cooperative; building security holding the elevator.
scene.bystanders: Three coworkers and a building first-aider. They confirm the patient was at her desk, looked startled, said 'my heart's going crazy,' became pale and sweaty, then started 'not making sense' before nearly falling. They laid her on the floor and called 911. No CPR performed; she has been awake the entire time.
visualCues: Middle-aged woman lying supine on office carpet, pale and diaphoretic
Pill bottle visible on desk — metoprolol
Coworker holding her hand and calling her name; patient slow to respond
Smartwatch on wrist showing HR alert

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS finds a 56-year-old woman with known post-MI cardiomyopathy and prior NSVT lying on the office carpet, pale, diaphoretic, and visibly confused. Initial monitor shows a regular wide-complex tachycardia at 180 — monomorphic ventricular tachycardia. BP is 76/46 with chest pressure and altered mentation — this is unstable wide-complex tachycardia. The teaching point is rapid recognition that this patient bypasses the stable-VT pharmacologic ladder (amiodarone/procainamide/lidocaine) and goes directly to synchronized cardioversion with procedural sedation/analgesia.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 26, mildly labored, lungs clear in all fields
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Rapid weak radial pulse — too fast to count manually; cool, pale, diaphoretic skin; cap refill 4 sec
otherFindings: Regular wide-complex tachycardia at 180 — monomorphic VT pattern
SBP 76 — hypotensive
Substernal chest pressure 6/10
Altered mentation — answering slowly, repeating questions
Known prior MI with EF 35% and recent NSVT — high pretest probability for VT
Unstable wide-complex tachycardia → synchronized cardioversion indicated
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (slowly) My heart… it's doing it again. I feel really weird. I'm scared. Did I fall? My chest hurts.

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey; identify instability — chest pain + AMS + hypotension = UNSTABLE
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Presentation — instability defined by hemodynamic compromise, AMS, chest pain, syncope, or end-organ malperfusion.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patients will manifest elevated heart rate for age and may or may not also present with associated signs or symptoms such as palpitations, dyspnea, chest pain, syncope/near-syncope, hemodynamic compromise, altered mental status, or other signs of end organ malperfusion.

expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Adult Management 1–2 — manage airway, oxygen targeted 94–98%.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult Management: Manage airway as necessary. Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Cardiac monitor; defib pads on (sync mode pre-armed); 12-lead EKG
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia step 3 — initiate monitoring and 12-lead. Pre-arming sync minimizes time to shock.

expectedActions.shortAction: IV access ×2 large-bore proximal (sedation, post-conversion meds, fluids)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia step 4 — establish IV access; large-bore proximal preferred for any antiarrhythmic or sedation push.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check blood glucose — rule out hypoglycemia driver
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia step 5 — check glucose; rule out reversible cause.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Check blood glucose and treat hypoglycemia per the Hypoglycemia Guideline.

expectedActions.shortAction: Classify: regular WIDE complex tachycardia + UNSTABLE → cardiovert (skip antiarrhythmic ladder)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Regular Wide Complex Unstable — synchronized shock is the immediate intervention; do not delay with amiodarone/procainamide/lidocaine in unstable patient.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regular Wide Complex Tachycardia – Unstable: Deliver a synchronized shock based on manufacturer's recommendation. For responsive patients, consider sedation.

transitions.condition: Crew administers procedural sedation (fentanyl + low-dose midazolam) followed by synchronized cardioversion at biphasic 100 J; rhythm converts to sinus.

transitions.if: Treated as STABLE — amiodarone given instead
transitions.condition: Crew misclassifies the patient as stable wide-complex VT and starts amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes instead of cardioverting; during the slow infusion, the rhythm degenerates and the patient deteriorates further.

phases.name: Sedation given; sync cardioversion delivered
phases.description: Crew briefly explains the procedure. Procedural sedation given: fentanyl 75 mcg IV (75 kg × 1 mcg/kg = 75 mcg, max 100 mcg) for analgesia, plus midazolam 2 mg IV slow push for amnesia/anxiolysis (small initial increment given marginal BP and elderly-leaning physiology). Pre-oxygenated, monitored, and synchronized cardioversion delivered at biphasic 100 J. Rhythm converts to sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with prompt restoration of BP and resolution of chest pain. Mentation clears within 30 seconds.
phases.triggerCondition: Pre-shock procedural sedation (fentanyl + low-dose midazolam) administered followed by synchronized cardioversion at biphasic 100 J; rhythm converted to sinus.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong regular pulse at 78 post-conversion; warm and dry
otherFindings: Pre-shock fentanyl 75 mcg + midazolam 2 mg given
Synchronized cardioversion 100 J biphasic delivered
Rhythm converted to sinus at 78 bpm
BP 118/72 — restored
Chest pain resolved
Mentation clearing rapidly
Patient drowsy but easily arousable
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (drowsy) I felt the medicine first… thank you. I feel a lot better now. My head feels clear again.

expectedActions.shortAction: Fentanyl 75 mcg IV (75 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg) — analgesia
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 6b — fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV/IM/IN/IO max 100 mcg; analgesia for procedure.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: For Moderate to Severe pain, analgesics include: Fentanyl: 1 mcg/kg IN, IM, IV or IO (maximum initial dose of 100 mcg).

expectedActions.shortAction: Midazolam 2 mg slow IV — amnesia/anxiolysis (small increment given hypotension)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Safety #3 — if cardioverting, consider sedation and pain control. Use small initial increment with marginal BP.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: If using cardioversion, consider sedation and pain control.

expectedActions.shortAction: Allergies, BP, SpO2 confirmed; capnography on (earliest hypoventilation indicator)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 7 + Patient Safety #1, #2 — capnography earlier predictor of hypoventilation; caution with opioids in hypotension.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Use of non-invasive capnography is an earlier predictor of hypoventilation than pulse oximetry if opioid medications are administered. Patient Safety Considerations: Administer opioids with caution to patients with Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) less than 15, hypotension, identified medication allergy, hypoxia (SPO2 less than 90%) after maximal supplemental oxygen therapy, or signs of hypoventilation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Synchronized cardioversion 100 J biphasic; reassess pulse/rhythm post-shock
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Regular Wide Complex Unstable — synchronized shock per manufacturer for unstable wide-complex VT.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regular Wide Complex Tachycardia – Unstable: Deliver a synchronized shock based on manufacturer's recommendation. For responsive patients, consider sedation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Post-shock: confirm rhythm, pulse, BP, mentation; rhythm strip + 12-lead
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Documentation — initial rhythm, all rhythm changes, time/dose/response of meds, monitor strips after each intervention.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Initial rhythm and all rhythm changes; Time, dose, and response to medications given; Cardioversion times, synchronization, attempts, joules, and response; Obtain monitor strips after each intervention; Patient weight.

transitions.condition: Rhythm converted to sinus; hemodynamics restored; transport for cardiology/EP evaluation; monitor for VT recurrence and post-sedation respiratory status.

phases.name: Sinus rhythm; transport to PCI/EP center
phases.description: Patient transported to PCI- and electrophysiology-capable receiving hospital with continuous monitoring. Pads remain in place with the monitor pre-armed in sync mode for any VT recurrence. Continuous SpO2 and EtCO2 to detect post-sedation hypoventilation. Bedside handoff includes pre/post 12-leads, joules delivered, sedation given, and history of prior MI / EF 35% / recent NSVT.
phases.triggerCondition: Rhythm converted to sinus; hemodynamics restored; transport with continued monitoring of mentation and respiratory status during sedation wear-off.

otherFindings: Mentation fully cleared as sedation wears off
No VT recurrence on continuous monitoring
BP stable; chest pain resolved
12-lead post-conversion: sinus rhythm, no acute ST changes, prior inferior Q waves
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I feel pretty good now. A little tired and sore on my chest where the pads were. Thank you both.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continuous monitor for VT recurrence; pads stay on; pre-arm sync mode
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Documentation — initial rhythm and changes; monitor strips after each intervention. Pads remain in place for any recurrence given known structural heart disease.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: Initial rhythm and all rhythm changes; Time, dose, and response to medications given. Obtain monitor strips after each intervention.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continuous SpO2 + capnography during sedation wear-off; reassess RR/mentation q5 min
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Pain Management 7 + Documentation — capnography earlier predictor of opioid hypoventilation; reassess.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Use of non-invasive capnography is an earlier predictor of hypoventilation than pulse oximetry if opioid medications are administered. Documentation of patient reassessment with repeat vital signs and patient pain scale assessment.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport to PCI/EP-capable hospital; bedside handoff with strips + dose timeline
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Documentation — full handoff with rhythm strips, sedation, cardioversion details and history of structural heart disease.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Cardioversion times, synchronization, attempts, joules, and response. Obtain monitor strips after each intervention. Patient weight. History of event supporting treatment of underlying causes.

phases.name: Treated as STABLE — amiodarone given instead
phases.description: Crew anchored on the wide-complex morphology and started amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes — a stable-VT treatment — without acknowledging the unstable signs (BP 76/46, AMS, chest pain). Three minutes into the infusion the rhythm degenerates from monomorphic VT into polymorphic VT, BP drops further, and the patient briefly loses consciousness. The teaching beat is that protocol explicitly defines unstable wide-complex VT as a cardioversion case, not an amiodarone case. The crew now must abandon the antiarrhythmic, deliver synchronized shock with what sedation they can give safely, and prepare for possible defibrillation if the rhythm becomes pulseless.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew misclassified the patient as stable and started amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 minutes; during slow infusion the rhythm deteriorates and the patient briefly loses consciousness — unstable VT now degenerating.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Carotid pulse only — radial absent; cool, mottled
otherFindings: Rhythm degenerated from monomorphic VT → polymorphic VT (still has pulse)
BP 58/palp
Patient briefly lost consciousness during amiodarone infusion
Amiodarone infusion stopped
Cardioversion now urgent before rhythm becomes pulseless VT/VF
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: (unable to speak — moaning to painful stimuli)

expectedActions.shortAction: Stop amiodarone infusion; recognize misclassification — patient is UNSTABLE
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Safety #1 — only one antidysrhythmic at a time; instability requires cardioversion not pharmacology.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Only use one antidysrhythmic at a time. If using cardioversion, consider sedation and pain control.

expectedActions.shortAction: Synchronized cardioversion 100 J biphasic immediately; pulse check after
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Regular Wide Complex Unstable — sync shock is the indicated treatment; mentation now obtunded so no pre-shock benzo needed for amnesia.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Regular Wide Complex Tachycardia – Unstable: Deliver a synchronized shock based on manufacturer's recommendation. For responsive patients, consider sedation.

expectedActions.shortAction: BVM with O2 to support RR 8 and SpO2 86; suction ready; reposition airway
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Adult Management 1–2 — manage airway and oxygen as appropriate; obtunded patient requires assisted ventilation.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult Management: Manage airway as necessary. Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Pads in defib mode ready; if pulseless VT/VF, defibrillate per Cardiac Arrest protocol
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Patient Safety #4 + Cardiac Arrest cross-reference — be prepared for deterioration to pulseless rhythm; configure monitor for VT/VF alert.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Consider placing defibrillator pads on high-risk patients. Consider configuring monitor/defibrillator to allow automatic VT/VF alert.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document misclassification; debrief: unstable WCT bypasses antiarrhythmic ladder
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Tachycardia Performance Measures — correct identification and treatment for instability is explicitly tracked.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: Time to clinical improvement from patient contact. Correct medication(s) and dose given for patient condition, age, and weight. Correct cardioversion joules delivered given patient weight and/or condition. Use of sedation for responsive patient.
